content: Imagine a factory where instructions for making a specific product are sent from the headquarters to the production floor. In cells, messenger RNA, or mRNA, plays a similar role. It carries instructions from the cell's "headquarters" (the DNA in the nucleus) to the "production floor" (the rest of the cell) where proteins are made. The process starts when a segment of DNA is copied into a molecule of mRNA. This mRNA molecule then travels out of the nucleus and into the cell, where it serves as a blueprint for building a specific protein. The cell's machinery reads the instructions on the mRNA and uses them to assemble the correct amino acids into the desired protein. In this way, mRNA acts as a crucial messenger, ensuring that the cell makes the right proteins at the right time, much like a factory producing goods according to precise specifications.
